text: The Sonoran Desert region of northern Mexico in what is now the state of Sonora and the U.S. state of Arizona was also part of Jesuit missionary activities. It was in this region that, in the early eighteenth century, large numbers of German-speaking missionaries entered the extensive system of missions that had been set up by the pioneering Catholic missionary, Padre Eusebio Kino (d. 1711) to bring people to the Gospel. The Jesuits also did a huge amount of research on the region, leaving behind many valuable accounts of their missionary and scientific work. They were expelled from Mexico in 1767 and banned worldwide in 1783 but, while they existed, they were arguably the most successful of the missionaries among the indigenous peoples of Mexico. The Jesuit missionary accounts are, for historians, anthropologists, theologians and scientists, an unparalleled rich and fascinating source of historical, anthropological, theological and scientific knowledge, unparalleled in missionary history.
explanation: Reads like an encyclopedia-style historical summary with plausible details. It contains a factual error (the Jesuits were suppressed in 1773, not 1783), which AI-generated text sometimes exhibits.
